word_list.word: anger and haste hinder good counsel
word_list.phonetic_text: æn.gər ænd heɪst hɪn.dɜr gʊd kaʊn.səl
word_list.phonetic_am_text: ān.gēr ānd heist hin.dēr good kaoon.sēl
word_list.pronunciation: ˈæŋɡɚɹ ænd hˈeɪst hˈɪndɚ ɡˈʊd kˈaʊnsəl

senses.definition: used to suggest that making decisions or taking action when one is angry or in a hurry can lead to poor judgment and mistakes

word_list.word: draw not your bow till your arrow is fixed
word_list.pronunciation: dɹˈɔː nˌɑːt jʊɹ bˈoʊ tˈɪl jʊɹ ˈæɹoʊ ɪz fˈɪkst

senses.definition: used to imply that rushing into action without proper preparation or planning can lead to failure or negative consequences

examples.x: The supervisor advised the team to double-check their work before presenting it to the client, reminding them of the saying 'draw not your bow till your arrow is fixed' to avoid any mistakes or miscommunications.

senses.definition: used to imply that rushing into action without proper planning or consideration can lead to mistakes or setbacks that ultimately slow down progress
used to suggest that being patient and respectful of others' possessions and affairs is important, and rushing or interfering without permission can lead to negative consequences
used to suggest that it is important to move forward quickly and efficiently, but not at the cost of making hasty or impulsive decisions

word_list.word: the sharper the storm, the sooner it is over
word_list.pronunciation: ðə ʃˈɑːɹpɚ ðə stˈoːɹm ðə sˈuːnɚɹ ɪt ɪz ˈoʊvɚ

senses.definition: used to imply that the more intense a difficult situation or problem is, the quicker it will pass, encouraging perseverance and patience in the face of adversity

word_list.word: haste trips over its own heels
word_list.pronunciation: hˈeɪst tɹˈɪps ˌoʊvɚɹ ɪts ˈoʊn hˈiːlz

senses.definition: used to imply that rushing into something without proper planning or thought can cause more harm than good, and it is better to be patient and deliberate in one's actions

word_list.pronunciation: hˈeɪsti klˈaɪmɚz hæv ðɛɹ sˈʌdən fˈɔːlz

senses.definition: used to imply that rushing into things without proper planning or consideration can lead to sudden setbacks, mistakes, or problems that ultimately lead to failure

word_list.word: haste makes waste (, and waste makes want|)
word_list.pronunciation: hˈeɪst mˌeɪks wˈeɪst ænd wˈeɪst mˌeɪks wˈɑːnt

senses.definition: used to suggest that acting hastily or without proper consideration can lead to wasted resources or opportunities, which in turn can lead to a state of want or need

used to suggest that trying to do something too quickly or without sufficient care may result in mistakes and ultimately take longer to complete than if one had approached the task with more patience and attention to detail
